text: The surgeon identifies the duodenum and ileocolic vessels, sealing the ileocolic pedicle using a radio frequency device without clips or sutures. Dissection proceeds around the duodenum and pancreas, with eye sealing of the right branch of the middle colic vessels. A medial to lateral dissection is demonstrated in the right retrocolic space along Toll's fascia. To prevent ureter injury, the right colon is cleaved from the right periotolic area, progressing from bottom to top over the right colic flexure.

Following specimen resection, the surgeon performs a primary anastomosis using a stapler to create a side-to-side isoperistaltic connection between the distal ileum and transverse colon. The procedure concludes with a laparoscopic hand-sewn double-layer closure of the enterotomy.

The surgeon etches the peritoneal leaf of the sigmoid and proximal rectum from cranial to caudal. The inferior mesenteric vein is sectioned at its origin. The inferior mesenteric artery is closed and sectioned while preserving the left colic artery due to the absence of the left branch of the middle colic artery. The dissection of the left mesocolon insertion is completed, and the dissection proceeds along the holy plane, cleaving the mesorectal fascia from the precircular fascia. A top-bottom approach is used to avoid entering the precircular fascia.

The surgeon performs a lateral dissection between the perirectal visceral fascia and the lateral parietal fascia of the pelvis to avoid ureter injury. On the right side, the peritoneum incision at the base of the mesosigma is extended downward and forward towards the rectal pocket, with the sigma retracted to the left. The rectal dissection is completed by incising the peritoneal reflection, freeing the anterior rectal wall.

The surgeon extracts the specimens through a four-centimeter suprapubic mini laparotomy. The rectum is perforated along the suture line, and the head of the stapler is solidified by ensuring the colon has not rotated on the vascular axis. An anastomosis is performed using a technique similar to open surgery.
